title: Civic Anger That Doesn't Burn You Out
dek: When the news cycle tightens your jaw and floods your chest with heat, your fury is not a moral failing. It is a biological imperative, a boundary-restoring messenger that demands to be felt in the fascia before it can be channeled into the world.
bodyMarkdown:
When the screen flickers with the latest headlines—another injustice, another betrayal of trust—you feel it before the news anchors finish their sentences. The masseter muscles clamp down, almost imperceptibly at first, a subtle grinding behind your molars. It’s four in the morning, and the world outside is drenched in the damp chill of a slow rain, the kind that seeps under your collar and chills the nape of your neck. Your jaw tightens against that cold, a silent, stubborn fortress against the flood of outrage. This is not just information; this is a body speaking in its oldest language.

That heat you notice, low in the center of your chest, is the diaphragm bracing itself—pulled taut like a bowstring ready to snap. It’s the visceral core of your anger, the somatic pulse that refuses to be silenced. You might imagine your ribs as a cage, but more accurately, it’s a living bellows, alive with tension and readiness. It’s the body telling you that what you witness is not just a headline but a call to attention, a call for care and fierce presence. Your levator scapulae, those small muscles at the back and sides of your neck, stiffen in sympathy, a physical echo of your mental unrest. The body remembers what the mind sometimes refuses to admit: this is urgent.

The scent of your own skin, mingled with the faint trace of chamomile tea you drank two hours ago, fills your awareness. There is no shame in this somatic response. This is not pathology, this is biology. Resmaa Menakem teaches us that trauma is stored in the body, and Gabor Maté reminds us that anger is often the body's attempt to protect what is sacred. Your body is not betraying you by tightening, by burning; it is bearing witness. The trapezius muscle, spanning the shoulders and neck, knots up as if trying to hold the weight of the world on your shoulders. It’s exhausting, yes, and if ignored, it threatens to undo you. But this is the raw material of change, not a sign of failure.

You were taught that this fury—the kind that rises when you watch the news, when you hear the stories of injustice—was ugly, dangerous, something to be hidden or tamed. But listen closely: the body knows better. The subtle tremor in your forearms, the quickened pulse at the base of your skull, the way your breath shortens and then hitches. This is your civic anger, alive and awake, demanding to be seen and felt without shame. It’s not a moral failure to have this fire; it’s the body’s way of showing you where your boundaries lie, where your care has been triggered, and where your power lives.

The challenge—the real work—is learning how to hold this fire without letting it burn you out. To listen to the body’s message without becoming consumed by it. To honor the somatic reality of your anger as a vital, living force, not a flaw to be fixed. This is the first step toward a civic anger that sustains rather than depletes, that fuels resilience rather than collapse. The body knows this truth even when your mind doubts it. You have only to pay attention.

## The Biology of Outrage: Why Your Body Reacts This Way

You feel it first in the belly, a tensing deep in the psoas muscle, that thick, powerful core muscle tethered from your lumbar spine to your femur. The psoas is the body’s ancient alarm system, wired to ancient survival scripts that predate your ability to read or reason. When you encounter injustice—whether it’s a news story about systemic abuse or the slow grind of institutional neglect—your psoas clamps down. This is not a failure of your will or a sign of moral deficiency; it is biology, not pathology.

The psoas stiffens to ready you for movement, for fight or flight, even if your legs remain still on the cold kitchen floor at 2 a.m., the dim light from the streetlamp filtering through slats in the blinds. The muscle’s shortening presses your lower spine forward, squeezing your lumbar discs, and you breathe shallowly, your diaphragm locked high under your ribcage, as if bracing against an unseen blow. That diaphragm—your primary breathing muscle—holds the tension like a clenched fist inside you. It’s no coincidence that your breath feels stuck, the air heavy and hard to summon, your chest tight as the night’s chill creeps in through the window crack.

This is the body speaking its truth. The diaphragm’s contraction signals your nervous system that danger lurks; it prepares your voice for a shout, your limbs for resistance, your heart for a surge of blood. The diaphragm, the psoas, the trapezius across your shoulders, even the levator scapulae at the base of your skull—they all conspire to ready you for action when the world feels unfair. The jaw clenches—the masseter muscle tightens—as if your body remembers every time you were told to “calm down” or “not to be angry like that.” The body remembers. It never forgets.

Teachers like Resmaa Menakem remind us that trauma and rage are stored in the body before the mind can name them. This is why your outrage can feel exhaustingly physical, why it can burn you out if you keep pushing against it without listening. The body sends a clear message: Something is wrong. The muscle tension, the shallow breath, the heat rising in your face—these are not signs of weakness. They are your body’s way of saying, “Wake up. Pay attention.”

Gabor Maté’s work deepens this understanding, showing that chronic stress, often born from systemic injustice, rewires your nervous system. Your body becomes hypervigilant, the psoas and diaphragm perpetually tight, the breath perpetually shallow. The outrage you feel is the body’s way of trying to protect you, not destroy you. It’s a survival signal, not a moral failing.

So when your body reacts with physical intensity to civic anger, recognize that this is your biology, not pathology. You are not broken. Your psoas is not unruly. Your diaphragm is not dysfunctional. This is how a body primed for survival responds to threat—real or perceived. The answer is not to silence or shame this response but to meet it with respect, to listen to the muscles that clench and the breath that halts, and to find ways, with herbs or movement or somatic practice, to help the body release what it carries. Only then can your outrage become a force that fuels change, rather than burns you out.

## Civic Anger That Doesn’t Burn You Out: Resmaa Menakem’s Somatic Abolitionism

You feel it first in the back of your neck, that tightening of the levator scapulae, a subtle but persistent coil that won’t unwind no matter how many deep breaths you try to take. It’s the body’s ancient memory, that muscle knotting up in the cool dusk hours when the news scrolls by, relentless, a litany of injustice and heartbreak. This is civic anger, raw and unyielding, but it is not something to be crushed or shamed—it’s a force that lives in your fascia, in the sinew and the blood, the very architecture of your body. Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist and radical thinker, teaches us that trauma is not just a story told in words or history books; it is lodged in the body, passed down through generations, carried silently in the tissue beneath your skin.

Menakem’s work on somatic abolitionism insists that we cannot address racial trauma, systemic violence, and collective suffering without recognizing how these wounds live in each of us—in the psoas that contracts when you hear about a neighborhood burned down, in the diaphragm that holds its breath when another life is lost to brutality. This trauma is not abstract; it is physical. It is the way your masseter clenches when you try to swallow the rage, the way your trapezius stiffens, bracing for a fight or flight that never fully arrives but never quite leaves. What he names as “white body supremacy” is a somatic reality—patterns of tension and release, contraction and freeze, encoded in the muscle memory of entire communities.

Your civic anger, then, is more than righteous indignation; it is the embodied echo of centuries of trauma, a somatic language that demands to be heard and moved through rather than locked down in shame or exhaustion. This is not pathology, this is biology. When you push against your fury without acknowledging its rootedness in your body’s history, you risk burning out, wearing yourself thin like a candle guttering in the wind. Menakem’s approach asks you to slow down and listen—to the rhythmic pulse of your heartbeat, to the subtle shifts in your viscera as you engage with the news, with protests, with the conversations that stir your blood. The body knows what the mind cannot yet grasp.

In practice, this might mean sitting in the quiet of a late spring night, the air thick with the scent of crushed sage and moist earth, and tuning into the way your diaphragm lifts and falls with each breath. You might place a hand gently over your solar plexus and acknowledge the tight knots of anxiety or grief there, the way your shoulders feel heavy with the weight of collective suffering. This is work that requires patience and tenderness, a somatic tending to the fractures left by generations of violence and neglect.

Menakem’s insistence on the body as the site of abolitionist healing challenges the dominant narrative that civic anger is something to be managed, suppressed, or redirected into productivity. Instead, he offers a path of embodiment—standing firmly in your rage, feeling it in every fiber of muscle and fascia, and then allowing it to move through you without becoming a consuming fire. This is how you show up for justice without losing yourself, how you honor the legacy of those who came before without replicating the trauma within your own flesh.

So tonight, when the back of your neck tightens and your breath shortens in response to the world’s cruelty, remember: this is not your failing. The body remembers what the mind cannot yet speak, and in that remembrance lies the possibility of transformation. Civic anger, carried somatically and tended with care, can become a wellspring of resilience rather than a source of burnout. The muscle remembers. The body knows.

## The Herbal Support: Using Herbs to Support the Nervous System During Times of Civic Stress

It’s 3 a.m., and the city outside is thick with heat, the kind that seeps into your bones and won’t let go. You lie awake, jaw clenched so tight the masseter muscle protests with a dull ache, the back of your neck—levator scapulae and trapezius—knotted in quiet rebellion. Your mind churns with the headlines, the injustice, the endless cycle of outrage that threatens to consume you whole. This is civic anger, raw and necessary, but if left unchecked, it will burn you out like dry kindling tossed into a forest fire.

You weren’t taught this anger was a sacred fire; you learned it was ugly, dangerous, something to swallow or disguise. But here’s the truth the body insists on: your nervous system is running a marathon, not a sprint, and it needs allies—real, earthy medicine that speaks the language of your fascia, your diaphragm, your adrenal glands.

Enter the herbs, those old friends who hold the wisdom of the earth in their leaves and roots. Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) is one such ally, a nervine that doesn’t sedate but soothes, like a cool hand on the heated masseter muscle after a long day of clenching. It’s the kind of herb that Pema Chödrön might describe as tender toughness—softening the edges of your reactivity without dulling your clarity. Taken as a tincture ([Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20)), it supports the parasympathetic nervous system, encouraging your diaphragm to release its tight grip, letting breath come deeper and easier.

Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) is another stalwart of nervous system support, especially when your heart races with the weight of systemic injustice. It’s not a herb for the faint-hearted; it acts like a wise elder, reminding your body that the clenching in your chest—the pectoralis major tightening like a shield—can soften without surrendering the fierce resolve that fuels your activism. Resmaa Menakem’s work on somatic abolition speaks to this embodied resilience—the body holds trauma, yes, but it also holds the capacity for profound restoration.

These herbs don’t erase your anger. They don’t ask you to tamp it down or deny the righteous fuel that feeds your civic fire. They meet the biology of your nervous system where it is, cradling the trapezius and levator scapulae wrapped in tension as you scroll through the news at dawn, or pacing the kitchen floor when sleep won’t come. Gabor Maté reminds us that this is not pathology; it is biology responding to a world that often feels unsafe, unjust, and relentless.

When you sip a carefully prepared infusion of Skullcap or Motherwort, you are engaging in a somatic dialogue with your body. You are telling the diaphragm it can relax, the adrenals they can pause, the masseter it doesn’t have to grind down into exhaustion. Robert Augustus Masters, who teaches about the wisdom of the body’s emotional expressions, would remind you that this is a radical act of self-preservation—one that honors your anger’s essential message without letting it consume you.

So on nights when the city’s noise feels like it will swallow you whole, remember the herbs in your cabinet. They are not a quick fix, nor a way to neuter your fury. They are medicine—slow, serious, and deeply rooted in the earth’s own understanding of resilience. Let them be part of your practice, a way to carry your civic anger through the long haul, not as a burning ember that consumes, but as a steady flame that illuminates the path forward.

## Civic Anger as the Boundary That Holds Us Together

You feel it in your chest first, a tightening behind the sternum, like the diaphragm is bracing itself for a heavy load. It’s late afternoon, the sun slants low through the cracked window, dust motes catching in the golden light, and your trapezius muscles—those silent workhorses that hold your neck and shoulders—flare with tension. This is not just irritation or frustration; it is something deeper, a visceral signal that the collective boundaries have been breached. Karla McLaren, whose work I lean into when the fire of civic anger flares, calls anger the “boundary-restoring” emotion. She reminds us that anger is not a moral failing or a reckless outburst, but a vital messenger, a biological response that rings alarm bells when the limits of respect, dignity, and justice are trampled—not just for you, but for the body politic.

When you witness injustice—whether it’s systemic racism, environmental devastation, or the erasure of marginalized voices—your anger isn’t just personal: it is a boundary marker for the collective body. It’s the levator scapulae stiffening at the base of your neck, the masseter clenching in your jaw, the gut knotting in anticipation of confrontation or the need to protect. This anger is a necessary firewall. It says, “Enough.” It says, “This is not safe. This is not fair. This cannot stand.” Karla’s perspective helps you understand that your civic anger functions like the skin of a community, keeping the toxic elements out, demanding accountability, and drawing clear lines where they’ve been blurred or erased.

But here’s the rub: when civic anger burns too hot, it sears your own tissues from the inside out. The psoas tightens, the breath becomes shallow, and the body begins to store this chronic heat as tension or pain. This is the danger of anger when it’s unacknowledged or dismissed, especially for women taught to swallow their fury, to quiet the body’s alarms lest they be labeled “too much.” McLaren’s teachings give you permission to honor this anger as a protective boundary, not as a threat. She offers a map for listening to the anger’s specific message—what boundary has been crossed? What needs protection?—and then moving that energy through the body before it turns into resentment or burnout.

You don’t have to carry the weight of the world’s broken boundaries in your jaw or your shoulders. You can let the anger do its job: alert you, energize you, and guide your actions. When you feel that familiar heat rising in your chest, pause. Notice the physical sensation. Acknowledge the boundary that has been violated. And then, rather than letting the fire consume you, ask the body what it needs to restore that boundary. Maybe it’s a difficult conversation, a letter written, a protest joined, or simply a firm “no.” This is how civic anger becomes a tool for collective healing, a way to hold the line without losing yourself in the process. The body knows the difference between a boundary that protects and a wall that isolates. Listen to it.

## Embodied Practice: Grounding the Fire

It’s evening now, the sky bruised purple and gray, and the news cycle has left you vibrating with a low-grade hum of outrage. Your masseter is locked, your breath shallow, the psoas tight as a coiled spring. This is the moment when civic anger threatens to tip from fuel into burnout. The body is holding the fire, but it needs a place to put it down. This short embodied practice is not about extinguishing your anger—it’s about grounding it, giving it roots so it doesn’t consume the canopy of your nervous system.

Begin by standing. Feel the solid floor beneath your feet, the cold tile or the worn wood. Press your heels down, then the balls of your feet, then your toes. Imagine roots extending from the soles of your feet deep into the earth, anchoring you. The earth can hold this fire; it has held the fires of generations before you. Let the ground take some of the weight your trapezius has been carrying all day.

Now, bring your attention to your jaw. The masseter muscle, where so much unspoken fury lives, is likely clenched. Gently place your fingertips on the hinges of your jaw, just below your cheekbones. Don’t press hard; just offer the warmth of your hands. Take a slow breath in, feeling the diaphragm expand downward, pushing the belly out. As you exhale, let the jaw drop open slightly. You don’t have to force it; just invite the muscle to soften. Imagine the tension melting like wax under the heat of your fingertips.

Next, place one hand on your chest, over the sternum, and the other on your lower belly. Breathe into the bottom hand, feeling the psoas and the deep abdominal muscles expand. This is where the ancient survival scripts live. As you breathe out, make a low, resonant sound—a hum or a sigh. Let the sound vibrate in your chest and belly. This is not a scream of rage, but a somatic release, a way of telling the nervous system that while the world is on fire, your body is safe in this moment.

Repeat this breath and sound three times. With each exhale, imagine the heat of your civic anger moving down from your chest, through your legs, and into the earth. You are not losing your fire; you are banking the coals. You are storing the energy where it can sustain you for the long haul, rather than burning you out tonight.

When you finish, shake out your hands and arms, letting the levator scapulae and the shoulders drop. Notice the subtle shift in your body—the slight deepening of the breath, the softening of the jaw. The anger is still there, a vital, boundary-restoring messenger, but it is no longer consuming you. You have grounded the fire. You are ready for tomorrow.

## Sustaining the Fire: Clean Anger vs. Resentment

The rain has stopped, but the damp chill lingers in the air, seeping through the window frames. It’s the kind of cold that makes your levator scapulae tighten instinctively, a physical bracing against the elements. But the real chill, the one that threatens to freeze your resolve, is the slow creep of resentment. This is the shadow side of civic anger, the place where the fire stops warming you and starts turning your insides to ash. Sustaining the fire of your outrage without burning out requires a somatic discernment—the ability to feel the difference in your body between clean anger and toxic resentment.

Clean anger is a flash flood. It’s the sudden heat in your chest, the sharp intake of breath, the immediate tightening of the psoas when a boundary is crossed or an injustice is witnessed. It’s biological, necessary, and vital. It moves through you, demanding action, demanding a response. When you honor it, when you let the diaphragm expand and the masseter release after the initial shock, clean anger leaves you energized, clear-eyed, and ready to engage. It’s the fire that fuels the long march toward justice.

Resentment, on the other hand, is a slow, smoldering underground fire. It’s the anger that gets stuck in the fascia, the fury you swallow because you were taught it was ugly or dangerous. It lives in the chronic ache of the trapezius, the perpetual grind of the jaw at 3 a.m., the shallow, restricted breathing that never quite fills the lungs. Resentment is anger that has nowhere to go, so it turns inward, consuming your own tissues, exhausting your adrenal glands, and leaving you hollowed out and cynical.

The body knows the difference. Clean anger feels like a surge of power; resentment feels like a heavy, suffocating blanket. To sustain your civic fire, you must learn to track these somatic signals. When you feel the familiar heat of outrage, ask yourself: Is this moving through me, or is it getting stuck? Am I using this energy to restore a boundary, or am I letting it fester in the dark corners of my nervous system?

If you find the anger turning to resentment, it’s time to return to the body. It’s time to use the herbs—the Skullcap or the Motherwort—to soothe the frayed edges of your nervous system. It’s time to ground the fire, to feel your feet on the floor and let the jaw drop open. It’s time to remember that your body is not a dumping ground for the world’s injustices; it is a living, breathing vessel that requires care, rest, and release.

Sustaining the fire means knowing when to step back, when to let the embers cool, and when to trust that the fire will still be there when you return. It means honoring the biology of your outrage without letting it become a pathology of resentment. You are not a machine built for endless consumption of tragedy; you are a human being, flesh and bone, muscle and fascia. Tend to the body, and the fire will tend to the world.

## FAQ: The Somatic Reality of Civic Anger

**Q: Why do I feel so physically exhausted after reading the news?**  
**A:** Because your body is treating the news as a physical threat. When you read about injustice, your psoas muscle tightens, your diaphragm locks, and your nervous system shifts into fight-or-flight mode. This is biology, not pathology. Your body is preparing you to respond to danger, but because you can’t physically fight or flee the news cycle, that energy gets trapped in your muscles and fascia. The exhaustion is the result of your body running a marathon while you sit still.

**Q: How can I tell if my anger is burning me out?**  
**A:** The body will tell you. Look for chronic tension in the masseter (jaw), the levator scapulae (neck), and the trapezius (shoulders). Notice if your breathing has become shallow, restricted to the upper chest, or if you find yourself holding your breath. Insomnia, digestive issues, and a constant feeling of being "on edge" are also somatic signs that your civic anger has tipped from a vital messenger into a consuming fire.

**Q: Are there specific herbs that can help with this kind of burnout?**  
**A:** Yes. Nervines like Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) and Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) are excellent allies. They don’t suppress your anger; instead, they support the parasympathetic nervous system, helping to release the physical tension in the diaphragm and the jaw. They act as a somatic buffer, allowing you to feel your outrage without letting it overwhelm your biology.

**Q: Is it wrong to want to tune out and ignore the news?**  
**A:** Not at all. In fact, it’s often a biological necessity. Gabor Maté’s work reminds us that chronic stress rewires the nervous system in harmful ways. Taking a break from the news is not a moral failure; it’s an act of somatic self-preservation. You cannot sustain the fire of civic anger if you don’t give your body time to rest, repair, and restore its boundaries.

**Q: How do I process this anger if I can’t take immediate action?**  
**A:** You process it somatically. Use the embodied practice of grounding: feel your feet on the floor, release the tension in your jaw, and use deep, resonant breathing to move the energy out of your chest and into the earth. Acknowledge the anger as a boundary-restoring messenger, as Karla McLaren teaches, and let the body know that while the boundary has been crossed, you are safe in this moment. The action can come later; right now, the work is simply to let the body release the heat.

**Q: What if my anger feels too big to handle?**  
**A:** When the anger feels overwhelming, it’s often because it’s tapping into older, stored trauma. Resmaa Menakem’s work on somatic abolitionism highlights how collective and historical trauma lives in the body. If the anger feels too big, don’t try to process it all at once. Titrate it. Feel a small piece of it, ground yourself, and then step away. Seek support from a somatic therapist, a trusted community, or the grounding presence of the natural world. You don’t have to carry it all alone.
